In a detailed report,Next also warned of thechalleges widerUKeconomy,cautioningthatgrowth was likely to slow as 'employer tax ncreases, and their potenlmpct bricesand
ilterthroughintothe ompany".
Next said the rise in ational insurance for mployers "creates a isproportionate ncrease in the cost of art-time work in tores"whhm pasignificant roportionof mploymentacrossthe

industry. To highlight the point, Next included a table in its trading update which showed the national insurance changes on three levels of salaries.The graph showed that a £9,520 part-time national living wage salary will increase by 6.5 per cent, while a full-time wage of £22,310 would increase by 3.5 per cent and a £60,000 salary by 2 per cent.
Higher costs of employing low-paid,part-time workers could deter retailers from hiring, reducing opportunities for those
seeking flexible roles to re-enter the workforce. This risks undermining social mobility and getting more people back into the workplace.

The high street leader has added to a growing number of retail businesses,including AO and Currys, warning over price rises to combat cost increases. The British Chambers of Commerce said earlier this week that more than half of companies were planning to lift prices over the next three months due to cost pressures.
Asda trails  h  pack for  festive sales
Emma Taggart
Debt-laden Asda was the worst-performing supermarket chain over the Christmas period.

The private equity-controlled group suffered a 5.8 percent fall in sales in the 12 weeks to December 29, driving its market share down from l3.5 per cent to 12.5 per cent, data from the market research group Kantar showed.
Asda said it had seen improvements to its sales and market share in the four weeks to December 29 compared with the same period in 2023.

The Leeds-based grocer has struggled with slowing sales since it was bought by the private equity firm TDR and the billionaire Issa brothers in 202l in a highly leveraged £6.8 billion takeover deal. Asda's large debt pile has also prevented it from keeping up with lower-priced competitors such as the German discounters Aldi and Lidl.

In November Lord Rose of Monewden, then chairman of Asda, admitted that the group had "not been as sharp on our trading stance as we should have been … we have lost a bit of market share and a bit of momentum".

The fall in Asda's market share comes as infation across supermarkets climbed to its highest level since March last year. Grocery price inflation rose to 3.7 per cent last month, up from 2.6 per cent in Noembr accoring to Kantar.
a cent in the four weeks to December 29,as households spent an average of £460on groceries during the period.
a and consumer insight at Kantar, said:"It was a solid Christmas at the supermarkets with sales surpassing £3 billion during the four weeks of December for the first time ever."专

Tesco continued to solidify its posi tion as Britain's biggest supermarket chain as it grew its market share by 0.8 percentage points to 28.5 per cent in 12 weeks to December 29. Sales rose 5 per cent in the festive period.中专

Sainsbury's reported its largest market share since December 2019 at 16 per cent. Sales at Britain's second bigest supermarket group outpaced the wider market to rise 3.5 per cent in the 12 weeks to December 29.
The German discount chains Aldi and Lidl both recorded their highest market share for the festive quarter, at 10 per cent and 7.3 per cent respectively.
Telegraph  s h  sale  O quicker
mes Hurley
ce  f ph di roup hs ou o e f ar e newspaper's editor warned of an nevitable sense ofdrift"overthe otracted auction of the business.
Anna Jones, who took over last year nid a pontal ae of Te Tlegraph,lmitted that the group's executives ought that an acquisition of the r  e  e d isted" by now.
he business as a result of a sale
process, which was called "the newspaper auction from hell"  by the Columbia Journalism Review, but said staff were able to "shut out the noise".

Jones's intervention followed a new year message to staff from Chris Evans,The Telegraph's editor-in-chief, in which he described the situation as unsatisfactory. "Last year was supposed to be the year when The Telegraph was sold. We were told we would be sold by the summer, then by autumn and, at the latest, by Christmas," he wrote. "But we don't know when it might be sold."
a]to what we can do in these circumstances but we're going to try to do everything we can to recover momentum and to defeat the inevitable sense of drift."

RedBird IMI, a venture part-funded by an Emirati royal, has been in control of The Telegraph's future for nearly eight months but is struggling to find a buyer. The fund is seeking full repayment of the money it initially invested in the  debt secured against  The Telegraph and The Spectator, its sister publication, which was part of a plan for a full takeover of the titles.

Ministers blocked its attempts to convert its debt into equity ownership amid concerns about foreign powers buying UK media assets. RedBird IMI sold The Spectator to Paul Marshall, the GB News owner, for £lo0 million but is seeking to recoup a total of £600 million.Lord Saatchi said RedBird had "overpaid" when his £350 million offer for The Telegraph was rejected in August.
a.from senior politicians for Lisa Nandy,the culture secretary, to intervene.
a."The Telegraph Media Group has been for sale for l8 months now and I've been CEO for almost a year. To be candid, we thought it would be done and dusted within six months."

Jones, a former chief executive of Hearst Magazines, added: "As someone who's been through these processes before and understands how challenging it can be … I remind everyone that yes there's lots of pressure but not all of it is real … Listening to too many outside voices can become exhausting so we've been mindful to shut out the noise and focus on creating our own destiny."
Wolfson tidings spell  trouble ahead
BUSINESS COMMENTARY Alistair Osborne

C of Aspley always that hristmas traditions his festive allows fair.Guise figures Lord Take to the one Wolfson ahead of parade aren't just about every other retailer.How are the rest supposed to keep up with the Next boss:a best-in-class shopkeeper,who once again has upgraded profits guidance?

Typically,Santa delivered for Next:full-price sales up an underlying 5.7per cent over Wolfson's nine weeks of Noel and enough jingling in the tills to nudge the full-year profit forecasts another £5million higher to £1.01billion pre-tax. As for the shares,they rose 4 per cent to £99.12.

So, all's well with the world? Not exactly..Wolfson's seasonal update was crowned in thorns:a looming £67million jump in wage costs,mainly due to Rachel Reeves's budget,and a warning that the UK economy is in for a "lacklustre” year.As he put it "We believe that UK growth is likely to slow,as employer tax increases,and their potential impact on prices and employment,begin to filter through into the economy." As ever with Wolfson,too, he brought tsome forensics to the subject —the sort seemingly beyond most chief executives, let alone government ministers.

The chancellor's budget raised staff costs in nwowas.Fisty upping the national living wage.Then by lifting the main rate of employers' national insurance contributions (NICs) to 15per cent from 13.8 per cent and also lowering the threshold at which the tax kicks in to £5,000from £9,100. And it's over the threshold that Wolfson's analysis is particularly telling.

Y Most employees in Next's 458UK shops are part-timers, working when the stores are busiest..They span mothers with school-age kids,students sand people between jobs.And when the tax changes take effect in April it's those people who'll be pricier to employ 一20 million more a year in Next's case.

.the higher living wageandtheNIC changes on a part-time worker earning g £9,520 ayear for a l-hour week tots up to 13.2 per cent of their wage packet.Contrast a full-time staffer on £60000 r: t 4 r cent. As he points out,the lower threshold "creates a disproportionate increase in the cost of part-time work in stores".

Ta biggest impact on poorer workers,Labour's typical constituency. As Wolfson nexplains:It it [the lower threshold]in principle.It's just the speed and scale atwhich these changes are coming inthat is damaging.1don't think they were well thought t through.It would have been more sensible to phase them in." Worse,they will “exacerbate"a labour market that was “already softening".This Christmas Next had 20 per cent fewer vacancies but "25 per cent more poe applving".

Next can cope.Wolfson will offset higher staff costs with productivity savings and a l per cent rise in prices. But, with consumer spending under pressure, he still sees lower growth this year —full-price sales up 3.5 per cent and profits 3.6 per

cent versus 2024-25's 5.5per cent and l0 per cent.True, he's renowned for downplaying his forecasts,even if he says:"It's better than the alternative".Yet,if a retailer as well-run and financed as Next sees trouble ahead,what does that mean for the rest of the sector?Probably not the happiest newyear.
A starter for six

B "chess master"are no obvious "blackjack card counter” and eing a self-styled "investor",qualifications for successfully sacking the entire boards of seven UK investment trusts. But,they should at least give Saba Capital's founder boss Boaz Weinstein a decent appreciation of odds. Hence,one thought::surely even he isn't expecting victory at all of them.

The biggest clue?That he's nominated one ofhis sidekicks to the board of no less than six of them. Who he?Well,not Max,Saba's chief happiness officer, a key member e hedge fund's seven-strong team, right down to his bow tie, even if he happens to be a dog— but Paul Kazarian, a partner & portfolio manager.Asyou hardly need one of the under-fire trusts,CQS Naturall Resources, to poit out:"Membership of six UK-quoted company boards is a case of 'overboarding',which most voting advisory bodies would consider to be a breach of the usual standards of '3

Y They would..Lob n Kazarian's existing director or trusteeships at three US-listed companies and he would be quite a busy bee, flitting around to endless board meetings.And that's before you consider just how conflicted he'd be,doing all that while still managing Saba portfolios.As CQS also notes:"Saba's materials state that Paul Kazarian has extensive experience as an invesment trust director. In fact, according to Companies House records, it appears hehasnever acted asa director of a UK investment trust,nor even a UK company of any sort."Starting out with six would be kind of pushing it.Even Weinstein must know that.
Reeves in a pickle

N oonesyet hitani or any But look other sort at the latestfrom iceberg of lettuce.the gilts market:yields on the 30-year have hit a 27-year high, up 0.07percentage points to 5.24 per cent. Even the mini-budget of Liz Truss and Kwasi Kwarcrash failed to hit such heady heights.

It leaves [Ya Rachel Reeves in a bit of a pickle.With borrowing costs rocketing, she's now tight on fiscal headroom, with all but £l billion of the £9.9 billion wiped out on Capital Economics estimates.The upshot?She could face,it reckons,the“nasty choice of breaking her fiscal rules or announcing more tax rises and/or spending restraint at a time when the economy is already weak". How wilting would that be for Reeves and her boss, Starmergeddon?
alistair.osborne@thetimes.co.uk
